definition: When analyzing an explicitly 'unprecedented' regime, confidence in predictions about its effects should decrease, not increase. Assigning 9/10 confidence to claims about a novel fiscal configuration violates basic calibration principles (Tetlock superforecasting). The logical contradiction applies a systematic haircut of 2-3 points to all agent claims built on historical extrapolation.
evidence: Tetlock superforecasting research: best-calibrated forecasters reduce conviction in uncharted territory
Current configuration has 0/74 pre-2022 precedents in U.S. data
Agents with highest average confidence were most frequently challenged in debate
Agents with lowest confidence scores produced the most durable findings
open_questions: What is the correct discount factor for novel-regime analysis (15-30% per Tetlock)?
Do understood mechanisms interact linearly or produce emergent behavior in novel combinations?
How should falsifiability criteria be specified for claims about unprecedented regimes?
